title: Mornings with Graham & Emmy
teaser: Warren pauses data centers post-storms; Saline pushes back on massive Oracle-OpenAI campus.

lines.text: Good morning and welcome to Mornings with Graham & Emmy, the Michigan Data Wire, your podcast on Michigan's data center buildout. I'm Graham.
And I'm Emmy.
It's Sunday, July 19, and we're digging into two big developments from yesterday that show how communities are pushing back on rapid data center growth.

Exactly, Graham. Warren just approved a temporary moratorium after storms knocked out more than 60 power lines, and over in Saline Township residents are fighting a $16 billion Stargate project already under construction.
Let's start with Warren in Macomb County. The city council voted yesterday to freeze new data centers while they rewrite zoning rules.
What triggered it so suddenly?
Those July storms exposed just how fragile the aging grid is there. City attorneys are now drafting the pause to give everyone time to figure out better protections.
Makes sense. How does this fit into the bigger pattern we've seen across the state?
It's the latest in a wave of local pauses. Townships are realizing approvals happened without strong rules on noise, power, or water use.
And what about the impact on residents' electric bills?
That's the big unknown right now. Data centers can pull enormous amounts of power, and utilities have to plan upgrades that eventually show up on everyone's monthly statement.
Water is another piece, right? Especially in farming areas.
Absolutely. These facilities need massive cooling systems. In places like Saline Township, locals worry it will drain aquifers that farms and homes rely on.
Tell me more about the Saline situation. It's a 2,400-person farming community, and this $16 billion project is already moving forward?
Yes. Residents say it will raise utility costs, erase rural character, and they've been vocal about it for weeks.
What's the Oracle-OpenAI angle here?
The project is tied to that big campus, and yesterday Saline Township lifted the $4.8 billion cap on tax incentives, handing the company what some are calling a blank check.
That sounds like a huge shift for local taxpayers. How did that happen?
The township board reversed its earlier limit. Now there's no upper bound on the property tax exemption, which could mean decades of lost revenue for schools and roads.
Who are the main players pushing back?
Local residents and some township officials who want stronger guardrails. They've highlighted traffic, noise, and the strain on the grid near Ann Arbor.
And on the other side?
The companies and some economic development folks argue it brings jobs and tax base growth, though the tax break changes the math for the community.
What happens next in Warren?
Attorneys draft the moratorium language, then the council will likely vote on permanent zoning updates. It's a temporary pause to buy time.
And in Saline?
Residents are continuing to organize, and there's a referendum coming up next month on whether to allow the project at all.
How common are these moratoriums becoming?
We're seeing more of them statewide as the pace of proposals picks up. Local governments are trying to catch up with rules that weren't written for hyperscale facilities.
What about the bigger picture for Michigan's grid?

Utilities are fielding questions on how they'll serve these loads without affecting reliability for everyone else. A public hearing is scheduled this week on the Microsoft AI campus in Kent County, for example.
That one is west Michigan's first major AI campus, right?
Correct. Neighbors there are already asking about added strain on power and water.
Shifting to quick hits, yesterday dozens rallied in Vicksburg against planned projects in Kalamazoo County.
Yes, concerns centered on noise, traffic, and utility strain. Organizers say more actions are planned if proposals move forward.
And in Dowagiac, residents filed a lawsuit today claiming nonstop noise from an existing data center is harming homes.
The case in Cass County highlights how some approvals lacked strong noise protections from the start.
House Speaker is holding up a bill that would set statewide siting rules and tax break limits.
Sponsors say the delay leaves townships without tools. It had bipartisan support before the stall.
Augusta Township voters will decide next month on a data center referendum after weeks of protests near Ann Arbor.
Traffic, noise, and power are the main worries there too.
Oracle won an unlimited tax break on its $43 billion Ann Arbor-area campus after the cap was removed.
That reversal means potentially decades of lost property tax revenue for local services.
New York's moratorium could affect Genesee County's STAMP project plans.
Local officials are watching to see if it loses its edge for jobs and tax base.
On deck this week, there's a public hearing on power and water plans for the Kent County Microsoft campus.
And the Saline referendum is coming up next month, so residents there will have their say at the ballot box.
Full sources and the live map at the Michigan Data Center Tracker.
We're back tomorrow.

episode_title: Warren Freezes New Data Centers After Storms; Saline Residents Fight $16B Project
